channel.title: NYT > Business
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Starbucks Reports Strong Store Traffic and Rise in Quarterly Sales
item.description: The chain’s chief executive credited its turnaround plan for improved U.S. same-store sales, but said higher gas and utility costs were a concern.
item.dc:creator: Julie Creswell
item.media:credit: Nico Schinco for The New York Times
item.media:description: Global sales for Starbucks stores open at least a year rose 6.2 percent in the latest quarter compared with a year earlier.

item.title: Prime Minister Mark Carney Says Canada’s Economy Is Expected to Grow and Deficit to Fall
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney’s budget update keeps to his path of diversifying away from the United States.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Sean Kilpatrick/The Canadian Press, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney of Canada arriving at Parliament Hill in Ottawa on Tuesday.

item.title: Deal Talks Between Pernod and Maker of Jack Daniel’s Falter
item.description: The collapse of the talks could pave the way for another spirits company, Sazerac, to open negotiations with Brown-Forman.
item.dc:creator: Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Scott Olson/Getty Images
item.media:description: Brown-Forman, which makes Jack Daniel’s whiskey, and Pernod Ricard did not give reasons for the failure of their merger talks.

item.title: Treasury Issues More Sanctions on Iranian Oil Exports
item.description: The measures aim to crack down on Iran’s shadow banking system and Chinese purchases of Iranian oil.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: “Iran’s shadow banking system serves as a critical financial lifeline for its armed forces, enabling activities that disrupt global trade and fuel violence across the Middle East,” Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a statement.

item.title: What Is OPEC, and How Does the Oil Cartel Influence Petroleum Prices?
item.description: The group of oil-producing countries supplied more than 25 percent of the world’s oil before the war in Iran. Its members have influenced energy markets through the years.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries
item.media:credit: Christian Bruna/Getty Images
item.media:description: The Vienna headquarters of the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries, which was created in 1960 as a way to control oil policies and stabilize global markets.

item.title: Supreme Court Appears Skeptical of Falun Gong Lawsuit Against Cisco
item.description: The court’s decision could have broader implications for lawsuits seeking to hold companies liable for international human rights abuses.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Jonah Markowitz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Members of Falun Gong during a protest meditation in Manhattan in 2024. The Chinese government has banned the group.

item.title: FCC Orders a Review of ABC’s Licenses Amid Feud Between Trump and Kimmel
item.description: The agency said the review was related to the network’s diversity and inclusion policies. But it came amid a fight between the president and the network’s late night host, Jimmy Kimmel.
item.dc:creator: Jim Rutenberg and John Koblin
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brendan Carr, the chairman of the Federal Communications Commission, has repeatedly threatened to take action against broadcasters.

item.title: For Many Pro Athletes, Post-Career Financial Worries Loom Large
item.description: The tennis player Mackenzie McDonald has earned well up to this point, but high expenses and a short career timeline have prompted him, and others, to search for a backup plan.
item.dc:creator: Cindy Shmerler
item.media:credit: Michael Stravato for The New York Times
item.media:description: At 31, Mackenzie McDonald has earned more than $7 million in prize money. But his recent demotion in ranking has him thinking about his financial future. “I’m very aware of my post-career life,” he said.

item.title: First Fully Loaded L.N.G. Tanker Since War Began Appears to Have Crossed the Strait
item.description: Liquefied natural gas transit has been effectively halted. A ship managed by Abu Dhabi’s state oil company managed to cross, said Kpler, a maritime data firm.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Gross
item.media:credit: Andrew Matthews/Press Association, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Before the war, about 20 percent of the world’s liquefied natural gas originated in the Middle East, virtually all of it headed to Asia.

item.title: G.M.’s Profit Bolstered by a $500 Million Tariff Refund
item.description: Because of a February Supreme Court decision, General Motors said it would receive refunds on some of the tariffs it had paid.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.media:credit: Brett Carlsen for The New York Times
item.media:description: A General Motors plant in Spring Hill, Tenn. The company is still affected by other tariffs — including those on imported steel, aluminum, cars and auto parts.

item.title: If OpenAI Is Missing User and Revenue Targets, Are They Falling Behind the AI Race?
item.description: The artificial intelligence giant has reportedly fallen behind on its own user and revenue targets, raising questions about its data center and I.P.O. plans
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Vivienne Walt
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sam Altman, OpenAI’s C.E.O.

item.title: Despite Trump’s Demands, His Fed Pick Is Unlikely to Get a Quick Rate Cut
item.description: The Federal Reserve is expected to hold interest rates steady this week as Jerome H. Powell presides over what is likely to be his last meeting as chair.
item.dc:creator: Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Todd Heisler/The New York Times
item.media:description: It is unclear whether Jerome H. Powell will leave the Federal Reserve once his successor as chair is confirmed. His term as governor runs until early 2028.

item.title: Stephen Colbert Gets Ready to Hang It Up
item.description: His late-night show ends next month after 11 seasons. He has lots of feelings.
item.dc:creator: John Koblin and Amir Hamja
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times

item.title: Graduates Reset Ambitions in Pursuit of First Jobs
item.description: Young people aiming to build careers are entering fields they had not considered to find their footing.
item.dc:creator: Sydney Ember
item.media:credit: Adam Amengual for The New York Times
item.media:description: A dismal job market has turned the spring commencement season into a bruising ordeal for many, including Sadie Parker, who will graduate in June from the University of California, Santa Barbara.

item.title: The World Needs Natural Gas Now, but the U.S. Is Exporting All It Can
item.description: Even the largest global supplier of liquefied natural gas can’t make up for the shortfall since the war in Iran cut off an important source.
item.dc:creator: Ivan Penn
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Brandon Bell/Getty Images
item.media:description: The United States is building more export terminals like this one in Cameron, La., but they will take months or years to complete.

item.title: What Elon Musk’s Clash With Sam Altman of OpenAI Is Really About
item.description: Mr. Musk’s lawsuit against Mr. Altman and OpenAI makes the case that all-encompassing greed is Silicon Valley’s defining feature.
item.dc:creator: David Streitfeld
item.media:credit: Illustration by Ben Denzer; Photographs by Eric Lee/The New York Times, Anna Moneymaker/Getty Images

item.title: U.S. Gas Prices Hit Highest Level Since Beginning of War in Iran
item.description: The jump on Tuesday of 1.6 percent was the highest percentage increase in more than a month.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Spencer Platt/Getty Images

item.title: Pentagon Can Temporarily Require Escorts for Journalists
item.description: An appeals panel ruled that the Defense Department can require escorts for reporters in the building while it fights an earlier decision that overturned many of the department’s press rules.
item.dc:creator: Erik Wemple
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: An aerial view of the Pentagon, the headquarters building of the United States Department of Defense.

item.title: Budget Airlines Ask Trump Administration for Billions as Fuel Costs Rise
item.description: A trade group for the airlines is seeking $2.5 billion to help offset the big jump in jet fuel costs since the start of U.S.-Israeli attacks against Iran.
item.dc:creator: Karoun Demirjian and Lauren Hirsch
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Megan Varner/Reuters
item.media:description: A trade group that represents low-cost carriers including Frontier Airlines is seeking $2.5 billion from the Trump administration to offset some fuel costs.

item.title: Supreme Court Appears Divided Over Roundup Weedkiller Case
item.description: The case could help determine the future of thousands of lawsuits against the maker of a popular herbicide over claims that it causes cancer.
item.dc:creator: Abbie VanSickle
item.media:credit: Martin Meissner/Associated Press
item.media:description: Bayer, the German conglomerate that acquired the maker of Roundup in 2018, argued that federal rules requiring herbicide safety labeling should bar people from filing state-level claims that accuse companies of failing to warn consumers of dangers.

item.title: Sergey Brin Moves to the Right, With a ‘MAGA Girlfriend’ by His Side
item.description: After once backing liberal causes, the Google co-founder has praised President Trump, donated to Republicans and spent $57 million to try to block a California billionaire tax.
item.dc:creator: Theodore Schleifer and Kate Conger
item.media:credit: Brian Snyder/Reuters
item.media:description: Sergey Brin and his girlfriend, Gerelyn “GG” Soto, at a White House artificial-intelligence dinner last year.

item.title: United Airlines Says It Pitched American on Merger but Was Rebuffed
item.description: United’s chief acknowledged for the first time that he had broached the idea of a merger with American, which was unwilling to consider it.
item.dc:creator: Niraj Chokshi
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Scott Kirby, chief of United Airlines, said a merger with American Airlines would have generated significant economic benefits for the United States.

item.title: Musk vs. Altman: A High-Stakes A.I. Clash Goes to Court on Monday
item.description: Elon Musk is seeking more than $150 billion in damages and a complete shake-up of OpenAI. The outcome could have big consequences for the artificial intelligence industry.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Ross Sorkin, Bernhard Warner, Sarah Kessler, Michael J. de la Merced, Niko Gallogly, Brian O’Keefe and Ian Mount
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Elon Musk wants billions of dollars in damages — and more — from OpenAI.

item.title: China’s Economy Starts to Show Cracks From Iran War
item.description: China’s strategic reserves of oil and natural gas have insulated it somewhat, but its manufacturing-based economy is beginning to falter.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Chinese-made cars waiting to be exported at a port in Suzhou, China, on Monday. China has sought to increase exports as demand has weakened at home.

item.title: China Will Require Meta to Unwind Acquisition of AI Start-Up Manus
item.description: The impact of the ruling was not immediately clear, but it could send a chilling signal to Chinese tech founders seeking to team up with foreign companies.
item.dc:creator: Meaghan Tobin
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Meta has described the two teams already as “deeply integrated.”

item.title: ‘Michael’ Fans Danced in the Aisles, Critics Be Damned
item.description: The biopic of the pop singer crushed box-office records on its opening weekend as admirers moonwalked to a movie that critics have savaged.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Ebrahim Noroozi/Associated Press
item.media:description: Fans of Michael Jackson gathered for the world premiere of “Michael” in Berlin last week.

item.title: The Rise of the High-Range, Less Expensive E.V.
item.description: Even as the electric vehicle market has slumped, there are more long-range E.V.s under $40,000 than ever before.
item.dc:creator: Francesca Paris
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Iran War Shakes Global Economy, but the U.S. Has Mostly Been Spared
item.description: In just eight weeks, much of the global economy has been knocked sideways. America has mostly been spared from the tumult.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Cohen and Ben Casselman
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Linh Pham for The New York Times
item.media:description: A farmer in Vietnam preparing plastic containers of diesel he had just purchased.

item.title: Oprah Winfrey Moves Her Podcast (and Books and Products) to Amazon
item.description: The multiyear deal with Ms. Winfrey signifies the tech giant’s growing ambitions in video podcasts.
item.dc:creator: Nicole Sperling
item.media:credit: Andy Kropa/Invision, via Associated Press
item.media:description: The move to Amazon marks a significant third act for Oprah Winfrey, who has dabbled in the podcast space with “The Oprah Podcast” channel on YouTube while pursuing other endeavors.

item.title: Immaculate Wilderness, Uncertain Future: Paddling the Boundary Waters
item.description: A proposed copper mine in northern Minnesota has become a battleground for politicians and environmentalists — and a pressing reason to explore the waterways.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Pearson
item.media:credit: Aaron Lavinsky/Star Tribune, via Getty Images

item.title: C.E.O.s Lean In on ‘Resilience’ to Manage Global Turmoil
item.description: The ability to stay calm and lead through any kind of shock is the new normal for corporate executives.
item.dc:creator: Jordyn Holman and Kim Bhasin
item.media:credit: Tasos Katopodis/Getty Images for Semafor World Economy

item.title: Supreme Court Wrangles With Police Use of Cell Location Data to Find Suspects
item.description: Geofence searches allow law enforcement to find suspects and witnesses by sweeping up location data from cellphone users near crime scenes.
item.dc:creator: Ann E. Marimow
item.media:credit: Steve Helber/Associated Press
item.media:description: A 2019 robbery at the Call Federal Credit Union in Midlothian, Va., led law enforcement officials to turn to a so-called geofence warrant in an effort to find leads.

item.title: The Rising Chinese Automaker Not Named BYD
item.description: Geely is challenging the giant BYD by adapting quickly to swings in demand and energy prices, seizing on interest in electric vehicles prompted by the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Keith Bradsher
item.media:credit: Chang W. Lee/The New York Times

item.title: Oil Rises, and Stocks Tick Higher, as Iran Makes New Peace Offer
item.description: Oil prices rose and stocks fell after President Trump called off a trip to Pakistan by two U.S. negotiators for peace talks with Iran.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Spencer Platt/Getty Images

item.title: Thom Tillis Is Prepared to Advance Kevin Warsh After U.S. Drops Fed Inquiry
item.description: Senator Thom Tillis said he had received assurances from federal prosecutors that eased his concerns, setting the stage for a key committee vote on Kevin Warsh.
item.dc:creator: Tony Romm and Colby Smith
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Thom Tillis in the Capitol this month.

item.title: U.S. Sanctions Zigzag in New World of Economic Warfare
item.description: With oil prices in mind, the Trump administration has deployed a haphazard approach to sanctions on Russia and Iran.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport and Ephrat Livni
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent at a Senate hearing on Wednesday, where he said the decision to extend the waiver on sales of Russia oil came after developing countries lobbied him.

item.title: Dwarkesh Patel’s Podcast Lets You Eavesdrop on the A.I. Elite
item.description: Dwarkesh Patel was a bored college sophomore looking for intellectual stimulation. Now he commands interviews with Jensen Huang and Mark Zuckerberg and holds his own with deeply nerdy A.I. researchers.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Wallace
item.media:credit: Aaron Wojack for The New York Times
item.media:description: Dwarkesh Patel in his office and studio in San Francisco. “He’s very much in the community, in the inner ring,” one tech executive said.

item.title: How We Traced U.S. Government Gold to a Drug Cartel
item.description: Three reporters followed supply chains to reveal that the U.S. Mint buys gold that comes from foreign pawn shops and drug dealers, then claims it is from the United States.
item.dc:creator: Justin Scheck
item.media:credit: Federico Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: The reporter Justin Scheck of The Times at La Mandinga, in Colombia. It’s a government-owned cattle ranch where illegal miners tear up the earth.

item.title: Rising Costs Are Causing Couples to Delay or Forgo Having Children
item.description: High mortgage payments, higher child care costs and economic uncertainty are making some people rethink their plans on starting a family.
item.dc:creator: Kailyn Rhone
item.media:credit: Lindsay D'Addato for The New York Times
item.media:description: Brock Goodwin, left, and Rilee Stewart had long expected that they would have several children. But after weighing all the costs, they decided not to have children at all.

item.title: Chevy Humphrey Is Running Experiments at the Griffin Science Museum in Chicago
item.description: Chevy Humphrey explains why the scientific method matters in business.
item.dc:creator: Jordyn Holman
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times

item.title: Targeted Hunts Were Supposed to Curb ‘Zombie Deer Disease.’ Now What?
item.description: In Illinois and other states, officials hoped that culls could halt the progress of chronic wasting disease. Now they are losing hope.
item.dc:creator: Jim Robbins and Julia Rendleman
item.media:credit: Julia Rendleman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Caught on a trail cam on the property of the photographer Julia Rendleman in Makanda, Ill.

item.title: How Do You Measure A.I. Firms’ Gargantuan Energy Plans? In ‘Bragawatts.’
item.description: One way for a company to stand out — or to intimidate the competition — is to boast, often without evidence, about how much power it has access to.
item.dc:creator: Erin Griffith
item.media:credit: Jackson Gibbs

item.title: Confusion and Fright at the White House Correspondents’ Dinner After Shots Were Fired
item.description: A sense of danger spread like a wave among high-profile politicians and journalists as an emergency unfolded at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum
item.media:credit: Salwan Georges for The New York Times
item.media:description: Secret Service members responding after the sound of gunfire at the Washington Hilton on Saturday.

item.title: ‘Michael’ is Expected to Collect $200 Million in First Weekend, Shaking Off Bad Reviews
item.description: Lionsgate said on Sunday that the Michael Jackson biopic would collect roughly $217 million over its first few days in theaters.
item.dc:creator: Brooks Barnes
item.media:credit: Lionsgate
item.media:description: Weekend ticket sales in North America for “Michael” reached an estimated $97 million. Box office analysts had expected about $70 million.

item.title: A Year Later, Trump’s ‘Most Exclusive’ Memecoin Event Is a Lot Less Exclusive
item.description: Even a Times reporter qualified for the event, which caused outrage last year for providing access to President Trump in exchange for investment in one of his family’s crypto ventures.
item.dc:creator: David Yaffe-Bellany
item.media:credit: Tim Enthoven

item.title: In Wine Country, Sales Are Down and Fraud Is Rampant
item.description: The industry’s murky supply chain has long attracted scammers and con artists. In the words of one expert, “Wine and fraud go hand in hand.”
item.dc:creator: Ian Frisch
item.media:credit: Joseph Gough

item.title: Thousands of EV Car Leases Are Ending Soon, Giving Buyers a More Affordable Option
item.description: The leases on hundreds of thousands of battery-powered cars and trucks will end in the next three years, and many will end up on used-car lots.
item.dc:creator: Neal E. Boudette
item.media:credit: Justin Sullivan/Getty Images
item.media:description: Analysts say lower prices of used electric cars and the surging cost of gasoline will encourage more people to consider models that they may have found too expensive earlier.

item.title: If On Shoes Are for Everyone, Are They Still Ons?
item.description: The company finds itself at a critical juncture that others have botched: How does it stay a brand for serious athletes while keeping up its breakneck growth?
item.dc:creator: Jordyn Holman
item.media:credit: Clara Watt for The New York Times
item.media:description: On’s LIghtSpray Cloudboom Strike running shoe — on display, museum-style, at the company’s Zurich headquarters — was made with a technology that mists the upper part of the shoe onto the sole.
